coredata.dc:title: An accurate and sensitive sensor based on the internal filtration effect for the detection of alkaline phosphatase and ascorbic acid by the Fenton reaction and dual readout signal
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Gaoya
You, LiuXia
Chen, Weishuan
Lyu, Haixia
coredata.dc:description: Ascorbic acid (AA) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in human serum are indispensable substances in the process of biological metabolism, and their abnormal contents are strongly associated with a range of diseases. Therefore, it is meaningful to develop a simple, accurate and sensitive assay for the detection of AA and ALP. Up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) have some unique characteristics, especially, the potential for background-free excitation by the near-infrared region (NIR) significantly improves the limits of detection (LODs). Moreover, sensors based on the inner filter effect (IFE) do not require covalent linkage between receptors and fluorophores or surface modification, thus the probe fabrication is simple and flexible UCNP-based. Therefore, a simple, selective, sensitive, and flexible sensing system for the detection of AA and ALP is proposed by combining the IFE and the cyclic signal amplification strategy. Under ideal circumstances, the LODs of ALP through the colorimetric and fluorescence method were 0.192 U/L and 0.013 U/L. The established sensing platform was applied to assessing ALP activity in human serum, and the detection results are close to those of commercially available kits, indicating its immense potential as an effective and convenient tool for monitoring ALP activity in clinical settings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase
Upconversion nanoparticles
Cyclic catalytic signal amplification strategy
Internal filtration effect
Serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NaYF4: Er/Yb@NaYF4 with core–shell structure
Detection of AA and ALP by fluorescence and colorimetric assay
Detection of ALP in serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21307013), the Fujian Province (2019Y0003) Science and Technology program, and the Fujian Province Natural Science Foundation (2015 J01044).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, the fluorescence spectrum and XRD of NaYF4: Yb/Er and NaYF4: Yb/Er@NaYF4.
Test diagram of UV absorption spectra (A), zeta potentials (B), fluorescence spectra (C), fluorescence lifetime (D).
Fluorescence spectrum (A) and UV–vis absorption spectrum (B). Experimental conditions: [UCNPs]: 1 mg/mL, [TMB]: 10 mM, [H2O2]: 300 µM, [Fe2+]: 250 mM, [Fe3+]: 250 mM.
Fluorescence (A, B) and UV–vis detection (C, D). Experimental conditions: [UCNPs]: 1 mg/mL, [TMB]: 4 mM, [H2O2]: 150 µM, [Fe2+]: 150 mM.
Fluorescence (A, B) and UV–vis detection (C, D). Experimental conditions: [UCNPs]: 1 mg/mL, [TMB]: 4 mM, [H2O2]: 150 µM, [Fe2+]: 150 mM.
Comparison of reported strategies and this work detection for ALP.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Ce (III)-GSH-AuNCs: Ce (III)-triggered aggregation-induced emission enhancement of glutathione-capped thiolated gold nanoclusters, WS2 QDs and Cr (VI): Tungsten disulfide quantum dots, PB nanocubes: Prussian Blue nanocubes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of spiked ALP in real samples.

head.ce:title: An accurate and sensitive sensor based on the internal filtration effect for the detection of alkaline phosphatase and ascorbic acid by the Fenton reaction and dual readout signal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Quanzhou 362000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Quanzhou 362000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, selective, sensitive, and flexible sensing system for the detection of AA and ALP is proposed by combining IFE and the cyclic signal amplification strategy. Under optimal conditions, the LODs of ALP through the colorimetric and fluorescence method were 0.192 U/L and 0.013 U/L, respectively. The developed assay was applied to assess ALP activity in human plasma, and its detection results were close to those of commercially available kits, indicating its great promise as a powerful and convenient tool for monitoring ALP activity in clinical practice.

Ascorbic acid (AA) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in human serum are indispensable substances in the process of biological metabolism, and their abnormal contents are strongly associated with a range of diseases. Therefore, it is meaningful to develop a simple, accurate and sensitive assay for the detection of AA and ALP. Up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) have some unique characteristics, especially, the potential for background-free excitation by the near-infrared region (NIR) significantly improves the limits of detection (LODs). Moreover, sensors based on the inner filter effect (IFE) do not require covalent linkage between receptors and fluorophores or surface modification, thus the probe fabrication is simple and flexible UCNP-based. Therefore, a simple, selective, sensitive, and flexible sensing system for the detection of AA and ALP is proposed by combining the IFE and the cyclic signal amplification strategy. Under ideal circumstances, the LODs of ALP through the colorimetric and fluorescence method were 0.192 U/L and 0.013 U/L. The established sensing platform was applied to assessing ALP activity in human serum, and the detection results are close to those of commercially available kits, indicating its immense potential as an effective and convenient tool for monitoring ALP activity in clinical settings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), commonly referred to vitamin C, plays a crucial role as an essential nutrient in the body and is often involved in the repair of the following tissues and the synthesis of small molecules in the body [1,2]. And the level of AA in serum is considered an important diagnostic basis for scurvy [3]. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an indispensable enzyme in phosphate metabolism and exerts a significant role in biological transduction and regulation of intracellular protein activity [4,5]. As a biomarker of some diseases, abnormal levels of ALP are often associated with certain diseases, such as bone disease, hepatobiliary disease, and prostate cancer [6]. Generally, ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) is catalytically hydrolyzed by ALP to generate AA that can be used as a reductive component in sensor designs, and thus can be extended to detect other disease biomarkers such as ALP. Therefore, it is meaningful to explore an accurate, convenient, and sensitive assay for determining AA and ALP.

Currently, various methods for the detection of ALP included spectroscopy [7], electrochemistry [8], and chemiluminescence [9]. Spectroscopy, especially fluorescence spectroscopy, has been widely used because of its simplicity, speed, and sensitivity. In general, organic dyes [10], quantum dots [11], gold nanoclusters [12], and up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) [13] are used as fluorescent probe materials. Among them, UCNPs have received more attention owing to their large anti-Stokes shifts, good penetrating power, unique optical properties, low toxicity, and high chemical stability [14]. In particular, near-infrared excitation light (typically 980 nm) is not absorbed by biological samples, thus eliminating background autofluorescence and improving sensitivity [15]. At present, the detection principles of sensors using UCNPs as energy donor materials are mainly based on fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [16]. Compared to FRET, IFE does not depend on the distance between the energy receptor and UCNPs, and therefore it does not require complex synthesis and modification to create a covalent connection between the donor and the acceptor [17]. The appearance of IFE is due to the conversion of analytical absorption signals into fluorescence signals, thus improving sensitivity and selectivity [18]. Chen et al. [19] constructed a sensitive platform for detecting ALP based on IFE between oxidation product (OPDox) of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and UCNP, achieving a remarkable detection limit of 0.03 U/L. Compared to the traditional OPD substrate, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) is neither mutagenic nor carcinogenic [20]. The absorption band of the oxidation product (TMBox) of TMB overlaps well with the emission band of UCNPs, resulting in effective fluorescence quenching of the UCNPs, so TMB is considered to be one of the candidates as substrates. Furthermore, it is well known that hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and Fe3+ are generated in the Fenton reaction between H2O2 and Fe2+, and ·OH are often used as strong oxidants [21]. If ·OH and Fe3+ are used for oxidizing substances at the same time, the oxidation effect will increase and there will be a cycle between Fe2+ and Fe3+. Furthermore, the formation of this cycle will also benefit the Fenton reaction [22]. Therefore, the developed sensing platform based on cyclic catalytic signal amplification strategy and IFE using UCNPs as fluorescent donors and TMBox as absorption acceptors will be simple, flexible and promising in biomedical analysis.

Herein, a novel dual-mode assay combining up-conversion fluorescent and colorimetric methods was proposed for the high-sensitivity and accurate detection of AA and ALP. First, Fe2+ reacts with H2O2 to produce Fe3+ and ·OH. Second, the generated Fe3+ and ·OH oxidize TMB to the TMBox. At the same time, Fe2+ generated after Fe3+ oxidized TMB can be recycled for TMBox production. Third, TMBox can significantly quench the luminescence of UCNP through the IFE. When in the presence of AAP and ALP, AAP is hydrolyzed by ALP to generate AA, which can inhibit the generation of TMBox and lead to fluorescence recovery, and the degree of fluorescence recovery of UCNP is proportional to the concentrations of AA and ALP. In conclusion, amplification and detection of cyclic AA and ALP signals can be achieved through redox between Fe2+/Fe3+, which promotes the application of UCNP in photoluminescence detection.

YCl3·6H2O, YbCl3·6H2O, ErCl3·6H2O, 1-octadecene (ODE), oleic acid (OA), ammonium fluoride, TMB, AAP, 30 % hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), Trihydroxymethyl aminomethane (C4H11NO3), acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer, AA, uric acid (UA) and cysteine (Cys) were obtained from Aladdin Bio-chem Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl), cyclohexane, sodium hydroxide, ethyl alcohol, and methanol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). ALP, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), glucose oxidase (Gox), urase, glutathione (GSH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), trypsin, pepsin, and lysozyme were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The morphological structure of the synthesized fluorescent materials was observed using various techniques, including Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, Talos F200i, USA), Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, SUPRA 55, Germany), and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Miniflex600, Japan). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was obtained by an infrared spectrometer (Thermo Electron Co, Nicolet 5700, USA). The fluorescence and ultraviolet absorption spectrum were collected separately using a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) and a Lambda 950 ultraviolet spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The zeta potentials of the obtained UCNPs and TMBox were tested using a Nano-ZS Zetzsozer ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., United Kingdom).

The synthesis of NaYF4: Er/Yb nanoparticles was referred to the literature [23] and appropriately modified. Initially, 236.6 mg of YCl3·6H2O, 77.5 mg of YbCl3·6H2O and 7.6 mg of ErCl3·6H2O were combined. Then, 6 mL of OA and 15 mL of ODE solution were put into the mixture. The mixed liquor was then stirred magnetically at 110 ℃ for 10 min and continued to heat to 160 ℃ and maintained for 30 min until all the salts of rare earth chloride were fully dissolved. After natural cooling, 10 mL of methanol containing 148.2 mg of ammonium fluoride and 100 mg of sodium hydroxide were added to the above liquor and maintained at 50 ℃ for a duration of 30 min, and then the temperature was increased to 70 ℃ to facilitate the removal of methanol. Subsequently, the mixture was further heated to 300 ℃ and kept at the temperature for 1.5 h. After cooling, the obtained mixture was centrifuged to obtain a nanocrystal, which was rinsed with ethanol and cyclohexane three times and then dispersed in 10 mL of cyclohexane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NaYF4: Er/Yb@NaYF4 with core–shell structure
ce:section.ce:para: Referring to the literature [24], the synthetic steps of NaYF4: Er/Yb@NaYF4 with the core–shell structure were as follows: First, 303.36 mg of YCl3·6H2O, 15 mL of ODE and 6 mL of OA were blended and stored at 160 ℃ for 30 min. After cooling to 50 ℃, 4 mL of cyclohexane solution containing NaYF4: Yb/Er nucleated nanocrystals were added, stirred for 30 min to fully react, and heated to 110 ℃ to remove cyclohexane. After the resulting solution was cooled to 50 ℃, 10 mL of methanol containing 148.2 mg of ammonium fluoride and 100 mg of sodium hydroxide was slowly injected and stirred magnetically for 30 min, then heated to 70 ℃ and kept for 40 min to ensure complete removal of methanol. The mixing temperature was increased to 300 ℃ under argon and kept for 1.5 h to form core–shell nanocrystals (OA-UCNPs). After cooling, the obtained mixture was centrifuged to obtain the nanocrystal, which was rinsed with ethanol and cyclohexane three times and then dispersed in 10 mL of cyclohexane.

For the subsequent application of UCNPs, the hydrophobic phase up-conversion nanoparticles need to be converted to the hydrophilic phase. To wash the OA on the surface of UCNPs, referring to the literature [25], 10 mL of cyclohexane solution containing UCNPs was added to the same volume of 2 M HCl solution, and the mixture was sonicated for 40 min and then centrifuged. The resulting precipitate was washed with water and anhydrous ethanol and subsequently centrifuged to obtain hydrophilic phase UCNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA and ALP by fluorescence and colorimetric assay
ce:section.ce:para: The spectral detection procedure for AA was as follows: First, 50 µL of 200 mM NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 3.2), 20 µL of UCNPs, 30 µL of TMB, 50 µL of Fe2+, and 100 µL of H2O2 were mixed. Then 300 µL of AA containing the different concentrations (0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15 and 20 µM) was put into the above mixed liquor and reacted at 37 ℃ for 20 min. Fluorescent spectra and UV absorbance were measured, respectively.

The spectrum detection for the ALP assay was performed as follows: First, 200 µL of ALP with different concentrations (0.06, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0 and 20.0 U/L) and 100 µL of Tris-hydrochloride buffer (pH 9.1, 10 mM) containing 100 µM of AAP were mixed and kept for 50 min at 37 ℃. Then 50 µL of 200 mM NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 3.2), 20 µL of UCNPs, 30 µL of TMB, 50 µL of Fe2+ and 100 µL of H2O2 were injected into the previous mixed liquor and incubated at 37 ℃ for 20 min. Fluorescent spectra and UV absorbance were measured, respectively.

Serum samples from human subjects were obtained from the Fuzhou University Hospital (Fuzhou, China). Before determination, samples were centrifuged for 15 min at 13,000 rpm, diluted 50 times with PBS, and then analyzed by the proposed method. The ALP detection procedure remains consistent with the aforementioned steps. Finally, serum samples with varying ALP concentrations were analyzed to measure the recovery rate. All experiments involving samples were conducted according to the guidelines of the institutional ethics board of the corresponding author, which had gotten consent from randomly selected experimental individuals.

To improve the luminescence properties of fluorescent probe materials, it may be an effective approach to prepare nanomaterials with a core–shell structure or hexagonal phase. The morphologies and structures of NaYF4: Yb/Er and NaYF4: Yb/Er@NaYF4 were characterized by TEM, XRD and FTIR. Fig. 1
A-C show that the average particle sizes are relatively larger and the fluorescence intensity of NaYF4: Yb/Er@NaYF4 is relatively stronger than those of NaYF4: Yb/Er, furthermore, the core–shell structure could be seen. These results indicate that NaYF4: Yb/Er@NaYF4 can be successfully prepared. Moreover, XRD patterns ( Fig. 1 D) show that all peak positions of the prepared material are similar and consistent with those of the standard card of NaYF4 (JCPDS No. 28–1192), exhibiting that they are all hexagonal phases, which could emit higher intensity of luminescence [26,27].

To verify the successful preparation of water-soluble UCNPs, the FTIR spectrums of UCNPs before and after removal of OA were observed. FTIR ( Fig. S1
, red line) of UCNPs before removing OA displays that there are characteristic absorption peaks at 2927 cm−1 and 2859 cm−1, attributing to the stretching vibrations of methylene, and there are characteristic absorption bands at 1569 cm−1 and 1452 cm−1, attributing to the stretching vibrations of –COOH. However, after removing OA on UCNPs, these peaks weaken or even disappear ( Fig. S1
, blue line) indicating that OA ligand has been removed by hydrochloric acid treatment and the surface of UCNPs have been transitioned from hydrophobicity to hydrophilicity, which is more suitable for use in biological sample testing.

To study the fluorescence quenching principle of UCNPs by TMBox, the absorption spectra of TMBox were measured in the presence and absence of UCNPs. Fig. 2
A shows that the presence of UCNPs has little influence on the intensity and location of the TMBox absorption spectrum, suggesting the absence of any complex formation between TMBox and UCNPs. Subsequently, the zeta potential of UCNPs and TMBox was tested with values of + 30.3 and + 26.3 mV ( Fig. 2 B), respectively. These data indicate that electrostatic repulsion may exist between TMBox and UCNPs, and thus the distance between them may be greater than 10 nm. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching principle of UCNPs by TMBox cannot be based on the FRET mechanism [28]. In addition, IFE has been reported to occur only when absorber absorption spectroscopy is well covered with excitation or emission spectroscopy of fluorescent material [29]. Fig. 2 C shows that the wide absorption band (550–750 nm) of TMBox covers well with the emission spectroscopy (620–680 nm) of UCNPs, therefore, the fluorescence quenching of UCNPs by TMBox may be based on the IFE mechanism. To further verify this principle, the influence of TMBox on the fluorescent lifetime of UCNPs was tested. In the absence of TMBox, the average life of UCNPs is 192.19 µs, while in the presence of TMBox, the average life is 191.51 µs ( Fig. 2 D). The fluorescence lifetime of UCNPs is almost constant, implying that the fluorescence quenching of UCNPs by TMBox is primarily governed by the IFE mechanism, as opposed to the FRET mechanism. This is because FRET will change the fluorescence lifetime of the energy donor [30].

In this work, a colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode sensing system for determining AA and ALP was proposed by combining the IFE and the cyclic signal amplification strategy. First, when excited with 980 near-infrared light, UCNPs produce strong green and red light (turn on) near 540 nm and 654 nm, respectively. Second, Fe2+ reacts with H2O2 to produce Fe3+ and ·OH. The generated Fe3+ and ·OH oxidize TMB to the TMBox. At the same time, Fe2+ generated after Fe3+ oxidized TMB can be recycled for TMBox production. TMBox can effectively quench the red light emitted by UCNPs around 654 nm (turn off). Third, when ALP and AAP are present, the former acts as a catalyst to facilitate the hydrolysis of AAP, leading to the generation of AA, which can effectively inhibit the oxidation of TMB and turn on the red light of UCNPs (turn on). In summary, an “on–off-on” colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode detection sensing platform based upon UCNPs was constructed for determining AA and ALP.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the cyclic catalytic signal amplification strategy, the fluorescence spectra of different systems were measured. It can be observed from Fig. 3 A that the fluorescent intensity of UCNPs, UCNPs + Fe2+, UCNPs + TMB, UCNPs + TMB + H2O2 and UCNPs + TMB + Fe2+ are almost the same. And only when TMB, Fe2+, and H2O2 are added to UCNPs simultaneously, the fluorescence of UCNPs can be quenched. However, the fluorescence of UCNPs is recovered after adding AA. Therefore, the detection of AA and ALP can be realized by determining the variation in the fluorescent intensity of UCNPs. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectrums of the above mixture were also tested. As shown in Fig. 3 B, colorimetric measurement can be performed only when UCNPs, TMB, Fe2+ and H2O2 exist simultaneously. Therefore, detection of AA and ALP can be achieved by using fluorescent and colorimetric spectra based upon the cyclic catalytic signal amplification strategy.

According to the analysis mechanism described above, under acidic conditions, the generated Fe3+ and ·OH by the Fenton reaction can oxidize TMB to TMBox, effectively quenching the luminescence of UCNPs. In the present of ALP, AAP is hydrolyzed to generate AA that can inhibit the production of TMBox, thus restoring the fluorescence of UCNPs. To obtain the best detection conditions, the effects of pH value, H2O2 concentration, Fe2+ concentration, TMB concentration, catalytic time, AAP concentration, and incubation time of AAP and ALP on the fluorescence recovery efficiency ((F-F0)/F0) were investigated, where F and F0 represent the fluorescent intensity with and without ALP, respectively. The effect of pH (2.5∼5.0, Fig. S2
A) and H2O2 concentration (0∼300 µM, Fig. S2
B) on fluorescence recovery efficiency were investigated and chosen as 3.2 and 150 µM, respectively. Fe2+ is the reactant of the Fenton reaction and the “bridge” of the enzyme-controlled cycle signal amplification system. Therefore, the influence of different concentrations of Fe2+ (80∼200 µM) on the sensing system was investigated. When the concentration of Fe2+ increases, the fluorescent recovery efficiency increases and reaches the maximum value when the concentration of Fe2+ is 150 µM ( Fig. S2 ). This is because more TMBox is formed with increasing Fe2+ concentration; however, this can still be completely reduced by AA originating from the reaction of AAP and ALP, and thus the fluorescence recovery efficiency increases. The concentration of TMB ( Fig. S2
D), the incubation time ( Fig. S2
E), the concentration of AAP ( Fig. S2
F) and the time of the enzyme reaction ( Fig. S2
G) were also optimized and chosen as 4 mM, 20 min, 150 µM and 50 min, separately.

Under optimized experimental conditions, the proposed fluorescence and colorimetric double mode method was applied to determine AA and ALP. Within the concentration range of 0.05∼20 μM, Fig. 4
A and C show that the fluorescent intensities of UCNPs gradually increase when the concentrations of AA increase, while the ultraviolet absorption intensities gradually decrease. Similarly, Fig. 4 B and D show that fluorescent recovery efficiencies increase linearly and ultraviolet absorption intensities decrease linearly when AA concentrations rise from 0.05 to 20 μM, the fluorescence recovery efficiency and the ultraviolet absorption intensity have good linear relationships corresponding to the logarithm of the AA concentration, and the limits of detection (LODs) (S/N = 3) are calculated as 0.02 and 0.33 μM, respectively.

Fig. 5
A and Fig. 5 B illustrate a linear correlation between fluorescence recovery efficiency and concentrations (0.06∼15 U/L) of ALP and LOD, which is calculated as 0.013 U/L, which is lower than those of some reported literature (as shown in Table 1 ). Therefore, the proposed method is relatively sensitive due to the catalytic oxidation strategy of Fe3+ and the low background interference achieved using NIR excitation. Moreover, Fig. 5 C and D show that the absorbances of the analytical system at 654 nm decrease slowly with the concentrations of ALP. Furthermore, there is a good linear relationship between the absorbances of the analytical system and the concentrations of ALP in the range of 0.06∼20 U/L and the LOD is calculated to be 0.192 U/L. According to reports from the literature, the concentration of ALP in human serum is 40∼190 U/L [36,37]. The colorimetric method in this work can also meet the measurement requirements of ALP in actual samples.

To further validate the specificity of this sensing platform for determining ALP, the effects of some coexisting proteins and enzymes including GOx, urase, HRP, BSA, trypsin, pepsin, and lysozyme on the luminescence signal were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3 , only ALP induces significant fluorescence recovery, and these coexisting related substances have little influence on ALP detection, although other coexisting proteins and enzymes are 50 times more than ALP. Since the fluorescence recovery effect of AA and ALP on the experimental system is due to redox behavior, the effects of other coexisting reducing agents such as UA, Cys and GSH were also examined and the results showed that they had little interference with the experiment. These results show that this proposed assay has relatively high selectivity for ALP analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the utility of the UCNP-based strategy for bioassays, the spiked recoveries of diluted serum samples were investigated. Table 2 shows that the recoveries measured by fluorescence method and colorimetric method are in the range of 96.6∼102.3 % and 98.7 %∼103.9 %, respectively, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are less than 3.50 % and 3.66 %, respectively. Furthermore, the test results of this work by the fluorescent and colorimetric method are close, indicating that the accuracy of the developed method is acceptable. Therefore, the proposed Fe2+/H2O2/TMB analytical system is feasible to determine ALP in serum samples and can be expanded to other biological analyzes.

In summary, a dual-model sensing platform based upon circulating signal amplification and IFE was developed to detect ALP using UCNPs as the fluorescent donor and TMBox as the absorption receptor. The proposed method has the following advantages: (1) UCNPs, as luminescent materials, have some unique characteristics, such as high photostability, large anti-Stokes shifts, and long excited-state lifetime values. Especially, the possibility of background-free excitation by NIR and the combination of cyclic amplification strategy can improve the sensitivity of the detection system. (2) The fluorescent quenching of UCNPs by TMBox is based on IFE, Compared to FRET, IFE does not depend on the distance between the energy receptor and UCNPs, and thus it does not require complex synthesis and modification to create a covalent connection between the donor and the acceptor. (3) The combination of dual fluorescent and colorimetric readout signals improves the accuracy and feasibility of the proposed method. (4) The low toxicity of TMB, Fe2+/Fe3+, and UCNP is more suitable for biological samples. Moreover, the sensor exhibits satisfactory results when utilized for measuring AA and ALP in actual serum samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21307013), the Fujian Province (2019Y0003) Science and Technology program, and the Fujian Province Natural Science Foundation (2015 J01044).

ce:section.ce:para: Gaoya Hu and Haixia Lyu designed research. Gaoya Hu and LiuXia You performed the experiments. Weishuan Chen analyzed data and wrote the paper. All author revised the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109517.
